headline: The Tigris runs black with ink as the Mongols end the Abbasid caliphate at Baghdad
summary: In February 1258 Hulagu Khan's Mongol army breached the walls of Baghdad, executed the last Abbasid caliph al-Musta'sim, and put the House of Wisdom to the torch, ending a five-century-old order at the centre of the Islamic world. In the same year the Byzantine restoration was gathering strength under Michael VIII Palaiologos at Nicaea, English barons imposed the Provisions of Oxford on Henry III, and a young Osman was born on the Anatolian frontier who would give his name to the Ottoman dynasty. The Kamakura shogunate under Hōjō regents ruled Japan, the Southern Song prepared for the Mongol storm, and West African Mali under Sundiata Keita's heirs was extending its reach across the gold-producing Sahel. It was a year in which a single Mongol offensive redrew the moral geography of Eurasia.

snapshots.text: The defining event of 1258 was the Mongol sack of Baghdad. Hulagu Khan, grandson of Genghis and brother of the great khan Möngke, had been dispatched westward in 1253 with orders to destroy the Nizari Isma'ili (Assassin) strongholds of northern Persia — which fell at Alamut in 1256 — and then subdue the Abbasid caliphate. His army, which contemporary sources reckoned at more than a hundred thousand horsemen and included Turkic, Georgian, Armenian, and Chinese siege engineers, arrived before Baghdad in mid-January 1258. After a siege of roughly two weeks, from 29 January to 10 February, the walls were breached; the caliph al-Musta'sim surrendered and was executed on 20 February, reportedly rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses so that no royal blood would be spilled on the ground. The city was systematically sacked over several days; the great libraries of the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) were destroyed and, in the famous phrase of later chroniclers, the Tigris ran black with the ink of books thrown into it. Estimates of the dead vary wildly, from Hulagu's own figure of two hundred thousand to later Muslim writers' claims of many times more. The Persian polymath Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, previously at Alamut, entered Hulagu's service and would later found the Maragha observatory. In Cairo the Mamluk sultan Qutuz was consolidating power and preparing the army that would halt the Mongol advance at Ain Jalut in September 1260. Along the eastern Anatolian frontier, in Söğüt, the future Osman I of the Ottoman dynasty was born (traditional date 1258).

In England the barons in the Great Council forced King Henry III to accept the Provisions of Oxford on 11 June 1258, a set of reforms imposing a permanent council of fifteen magnates on the crown and a triennial parliament; it was, in effect, the first constitutional constraint on an English king after Magna Carta and would spark the Second Barons' War under Simon de Montfort. On the western marches Llywelyn ap Gruffudd proclaimed himself Prince of Wales. In France Louis IX and James I of Aragon signed the Treaty of Corbeil on 11 May, settling their overlapping claims across the Pyrenees. In Sicily, Manfred, illegitimate son of the Emperor Frederick II, was crowned king at Palermo on 10 August and promptly excommunicated by Pope Alexander IV. In the Aegean, Michael VIII Palaiologos, having seized the regency of the young John IV Laskaris of the Empire of Nicaea after the deaths of Theodore II Laskaris (August) and his minister George Mouzalon, was preparing the coup that would restore Constantinople to Greek rule in 1261 and end the Latin Empire of Baldwin II. In the eastern Mediterranean the Venetians defeated a Genoese relief fleet at Acre on 25 June, part of the long commercial War of Saint Sabas. Salisbury Cathedral was consecrated on 29 September, its early English Gothic completed in a mere thirty-eight years. Across northern Europe, the aftermath of the 1257 eruption of Samalas in Indonesia produced famine and pestilence in England, Germany, and France.

In China, the Southern Song under Emperor Lizong (r. 1224-1264), ruling from the capital Lin'an (modern Hangzhou), was engaged in the long defensive war against the expanding Mongol empire. Möngke Khan personally launched a great southward campaign in 1258 into Sichuan, and the following year would die at the siege of Diaoyu (Fishing Fortress) near Chongqing — an event whose repercussions triggered the war of succession between his brothers Kublai and Ariq Böke. In the same year a Mongol force under Uriyangkhadai, having conquered the Kingdom of Dali in Yunnan, invaded Đại Việt from the south, sacking Thăng Long (Hanoi) in January 1258 before withdrawing under counter-attack by King Trần Thái Tông. In Japan, the Kamakura shogunate was in its mature phase: real power was in the hands of the Hōjō regents (shikken), with the puppet shogun Prince Munetaka on the throne of Kamakura and Emperor Go-Fukakusa reigning in Kyoto. Zen Buddhist and Nichiren teachings were spreading rapidly among warrior and commoner audiences; Nichiren, in his early forties, was writing on natural disasters as signs of political decay, and Rinzai and Sōtō Zen monasteries were being founded. The Goryeo dynasty of Korea, after two decades of ruinous Mongol invasions, was on the verge of the palace coup of April 1258 that would end the military rule of the Choe family and lead to accommodation with the Mongols.

The Delhi Sultanate in 1258 was in the last decades of the Mamluk (Slave) dynasty founded by Qutb al-Din Aibak. The sultan was Nasir ud-Din Mahmud (r. 1246-1266), a scholarly and pious figure best remembered for spending his time copying the Qur'an while real power was held by his father-in-law Ghiyas ud-Din Balban, who as regent and na'ib was systematically breaking the power of the Turkish 'Corps of Forty' magnates and would himself take the throne in 1266. The sultanate faced repeated Mongol raids across the Indus into the Punjab throughout the 1250s and would deflect them by heavy fortification, particularly of Lahore and Multan, and by absorbing large numbers of refugees fleeing westward from the Ilkhanid advance. In the peninsula the Chola state was in decline; the Pandyas of Madurai were rising to dominance; the Hoysalas at Halebidu and Belur were building their intricate soapstone temples; the Kakatiyas at Warangal ruled the Andhra country. Bengal was ruled by governors nominally subordinate to Delhi. In Sri Lanka, King Parakramabahu II reigned from Dambadeniya after the Kalinga invasions. In Southeast Asia, the Khmer empire under the last builders at Angkor and the Bagan kingdom of Burma were at cultural peaks. On the steppe, the Chagatai khanate ruled central Asia and the Ilkhanate under Hulagu was consolidating Iran and Iraq as a new Mongol successor state.

In West Africa the empire of Mali, established a generation earlier by Sundiata Keita after his victory over Sumaoro Kanté of Sosso at Kirina around 1235, was in the reign of Sundiata's successor, known in Arabic sources as Mansa Wali (or Uli) and in Mande oral tradition as Yerelinkon. From the Mande heartland in the upper Niger basin — the traditional capital is placed at Niani, though its exact location remains debated — Mali controlled the trans-Saharan gold trade from the fields of Bambuk and Bure, extended west toward Takrur on the Senegal, and northward toward the salt-producing Sahara at Walata and Audaghust. Mansa Wali is later remembered as having performed the hajj, part of the growing accommodation between the Malian court and the Muslim scholarly networks of the Sahel. To the east, the Kanem empire under the Sayfawa dynasty ruled the Lake Chad basin. In North Africa, the Almohad caliphate was in terminal collapse; the Marinids had taken Marrakech in 1269 and were consolidating Morocco, while in Ifriqiya the Hafsids ruled from Tunis, and in the central Maghreb the Zayyanids at Tlemcen were establishing an independent dynasty. In the Middle Nile the Christian Nubian kingdom of Makuria was under increasing pressure from Mamluk Egypt. On the Swahili coast, city-states from Mogadishu through Kilwa were flourishing as intermediaries in the Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade; Great Zimbabwe in the interior of southern Africa was entering its monumental stone-building phase.

In the Mesoamerican world of 1258, the great Toltec capital of Tollan (modern Tula in Hidalgo) had collapsed or was in advanced decline, its once-imperial hinterland fragmenting into rival successor states in the Basin of Mexico; the Mexica (Aztec) migration from the mythic Aztlan was reputed to be under way, though the founding of Tenochtitlan on Lake Texcoco lay nearly a century in the future (traditionally 1325). In the northern Yucatán the Maya League of Mayapan, founded around 1220 after the fall of Chichen Itza, dominated the peninsula from its walled city under the Cocom dynasty. In highland Guatemala the K'iche' and Kaqchikel Maya states were forming. On the north coast of Peru, the Chimu kingdom at Chan Chan continued the vast adobe-walled city that would eventually cover twenty square kilometres, while in the Cuzco valley the small polity of the emerging Inca — during what modern archaeology calls the Killke phase — was gathering the political forms that would burst outward under Pachacuti a century later. In the Mississippi valley, Cahokia near modern St Louis was in decline from its early-twelfth-century peak but still occupied. In the Southwest, Chaco Canyon's great houses had been largely abandoned in the mid-twelfth century after a long drought; Ancestral Puebloan populations had shifted north to Mesa Verde and were building the cliff-dwellings of Cliff Palace and Spruce Tree House in these very decades.

In the Pacific of the mid-thirteenth century, the eastward push of the Polynesian expansion was near its extreme limits. Recent archaeological and genomic evidence suggests that Rapa Nui (Easter Island) was first settled around 1200 by voyagers from the Gambier or Marquesas islands, so by 1258 the earliest generations of Rapa Nui society were establishing the ahu-and-moai monumental complex that would define the island for the next four centuries. The settlement of Aotearoa (New Zealand) by East Polynesian ancestors of the Māori is now generally dated to the very late thirteenth century, so 1258 falls just before or at the beginning of that transformative colonisation, which would produce moa hunting, distinctive kūmara horticulture, and the great pā fortifications. In Hawaii, permanent Polynesian settlement was already established, with intensifying dryland and wetland taro agriculture on all the main islands and the beginnings of the ali'i chieftain hierarchies. In western Polynesia — Samoa, Tonga, Fiji — long-established chiefdoms continued their sophisticated maritime networks. In Australia, hundreds of Aboriginal nations continued their tens-of-thousands-of-years-old traditions of land management, ceremony, and long-distance exchange; specific dated events do not survive from these oral societies for this year, but the archaeological record documents dense and continuous occupation across the continent. In New Guinea highland farmers cultivated taro, sweet potato, and sugarcane in intensively drained valley systems.
